questions.stem: Water has density $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. Taking $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the gauge pressure at a depth of $2.0\ \text{m}$ below the free surface?
questions.explanation: Hydrostatic gauge pressure is $p = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 2.0 = 1.96 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$. The value $9.80 \times 10^{3}$ uses $h = 1\ \text{m}$, and $1.96 \times 10^{5}$ slips a power of ten as if $h = 20\ \text{m}$. The trap $2.00 \times 10^{3}$ multiplies $\rho h$ without $g$.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: In a static fluid at rest under gravity, how does the pressure vary with position?
options: It increases with horizontal distance in the direction of $g$.
It decreases with depth below the free surface.
It increases with depth and is the same at all points on a given horizontal level.
It is the same at all points in the fluid.
questions.explanation: Hydrostatic equilibrium gives $\nabla p = \rho \mathbf{g}$, so pressure changes only in the vertical direction: it rises with depth as $p = p_0 + \rho g h$ and is constant across any horizontal plane (a level surface is an isobar). It does not vary horizontally, is not uniform everywhere, and increases rather than decreases with depth.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A diver descends in seawater of density $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. Using $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the gauge pressure at a depth of $20\ \text{m}$?
questions.explanation: Gauge pressure is $p = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.8 \times 20 = 2.009 \times 10^{5} \approx 2.01 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$. Using $h = 10\ \text{m}$ gives $1.00 \times 10^{5}$, and $2.01 \times 10^{4}$ drops a power of ten. The value $4.02 \times 10^{5}$ doubles the depth to $40\ \text{m}$.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: At a point $5.0\ \text{m}$ below the surface of a freshwater lake the atmospheric pressure above the surface is $1.013 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$. Taking $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the absolute pressure at that depth?
questions.explanation: Absolute pressure adds atmospheric to the hydrostatic part: $p = p_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h = 1.013 \times 10^{5} + 1000 \times 9.8 \times 5.0 = 1.013 \times 10^{5} + 4.90 \times 10^{4} = 1.50 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$. The value $4.90 \times 10^{4}$ is the gauge part alone, $1.013 \times 10^{5}$ forgets the water column, and $5.91 \times 10^{4}$ mis-adds the two pieces.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A mercury barometer reads a column height of $0.760\ \text{m}$. Taking the density of mercury as $\rho = 13600\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what atmospheric pressure does this correspond to?
questions.explanation: A barometer balances atmospheric pressure against a mercury column: $p = \rho g h = 13600 \times 9.8 \times 0.760 = 1.013 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$, the standard atmosphere. The value $1.36 \times 10^{5}$ omits the height factor $0.760$, while $7.45 \times 10^{3}$ and $1.03 \times 10^{4}$ misplace powers of ten.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: If water were used instead of mercury in a barometer, what column height would balance one standard atmosphere $1.013 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$? Take $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
questions.explanation: Set $\rho g h = p_{\text{atm}}$ so $h = p_{\text{atm}}/(\rho g) = 1.013 \times 10^{5}/(1000 \times 9.8) = 10.3\ \text{m}$. Water is about $13.6$ times less dense than mercury, so the column is about $13.6$ times taller than the $0.76\ \text{m}$ of mercury. The values $1.03\ \text{m}$ and $103\ \text{m}$ are off by a factor of ten.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A U-tube manometer connected to a gas vessel shows a mercury height difference of $0.10\ \text{m}$, with the gas side lower. Taking $\rho_{\text{Hg}} = 13600\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the gauge pressure of the gas?
questions.explanation: The manometer reads the gauge pressure directly as the head of the difference: $p = \rho g \Delta h = 13600 \times 9.8 \times 0.10 = 1.33 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$. The value $1.36 \times 10^{3}$ drops a power of ten, $6.66 \times 10^{3}$ uses $\Delta h = 0.05\ \text{m}$, and $1.013 \times 10^{5}$ confuses gauge with one full atmosphere.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: Two points in a connected body of still water lie at depths $1.0\ \text{m}$ and $4.0\ \text{m}$ below the surface. Taking $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the pressure difference between them?
questions.explanation: Only the depth difference matters: $\Delta p = \rho g \Delta h = 1000 \times 9.8 \times (4.0 - 1.0) = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 3.0 = 2.94 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$. Using the full $4.0\ \text{m}$ gives $3.92 \times 10^{4}$, using $5.0\ \text{m}$ gives $4.90 \times 10^{4}$, and $9.80 \times 10^{3}$ uses only $1.0\ \text{m}$.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A block of volume $2.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$ is fully submerged and held under water of density $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. Using $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the buoyant force on it?
questions.explanation: Archimedes' principle gives $F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 19.6\ \text{N}$, the weight of the displaced water. The value $9.8\ \text{N}$ uses half the volume, $196\ \text{N}$ slips a power of ten, and $2.0\ \text{N}$ multiplies $\rho V$ without $g$.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: Archimedes' principle states that the buoyant force on a body immersed in a fluid equals which of the following?
options: The weight of the fluid the body would hold if hollow.
The pressure at the centroid times the body's surface area.
The weight of the fluid displaced by the body.
The weight of the body itself.
questions.explanation: The net upward force from the surrounding pressure distribution equals the weight of the fluid displaced, $F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{disp}}$. It does not depend on the body's own weight or material, and it is not simply one pressure times an area because pressure varies over the surface.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A solid object weighs $50\ \text{N}$ in air and $30\ \text{N}$ when fully submerged in water. Taking $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the volume of the object?
questions.explanation: The apparent weight loss equals the buoyant force: $F_B = 50 - 30 = 20\ \text{N}$. Then $V = F_B/(\rho g) = 20/(1000 \times 9.8) = 2.04 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$. Using the air weight $50\ \text{N}$ as the buoyant force gives $5.10 \times 10^{-3}$, the water weight $30\ \text{N}$ gives $3.06 \times 10^{-3}$, and the sum $80\ \text{N}$ gives $8.16 \times 10^{-3}$.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: Using the data of the previous setup, an object weighing $50\ \text{N}$ in air loses $20\ \text{N}$ of apparent weight in water. What is the average density of the object?
questions.explanation: The object's weight and the displaced water's weight share the same volume, so $\rho_{\text{obj}}/\rho_{\text{water}} = W_{\text{air}}/F_B = 50/20 = 2.5$. Hence $\rho_{\text{obj}} = 2.5 \times 1000 = 2500\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. The value $1670$ uses the ratio $50/30$, $1000$ forgets the object is denser than water, and $5000$ doubles the correct ratio.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A block of wood of density $600\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ floats in water of density $1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. What fraction of its volume is submerged?
questions.explanation: For a floating body the displaced fluid weight balances the body weight, so the submerged fraction is $\rho_{\text{body}}/\rho_{\text{fluid}} = 600/1000 = 0.60$. The value $0.40$ is the fraction above water, $0.67$ inverts to $1000/600$ truncated, and $1.00$ would mean fully submerged.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: An iceberg of density $917\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ floats in seawater of density $1025\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. What fraction of its volume lies below the waterline?
questions.explanation: The submerged fraction is $\rho_{\text{ice}}/\rho_{\text{sea}} = 917/1025 = 0.895$. So about $89.5\%$ is underwater and roughly $10.5\%$ shows above the surface. The value $0.917$ wrongly divides by pure water of density $1000$, $0.105$ is the exposed fraction, and $1.118$ inverts the ratio.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A barge floats in fresh water of density $1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. When loaded, the total mass of barge plus cargo is $500\ \text{kg}$. What volume of water does it displace while floating? Use $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
questions.explanation: For floating, the displaced water mass equals the floating mass: $\rho V = m$, so $V = m/\rho = 500/1000 = 0.50\ \text{m}^3$. The factor $g$ cancels because it appears on both the weight and the buoyant force. The other options misplace the decimal by powers of ten.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A closed tank holds oil of density $800\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ to a depth of $1.5\ \text{m}$, with the air above the oil held at a gauge pressure of $1.0 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$. Taking $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the gauge pressure at the bottom of the oil?
questions.explanation: Add the surface gauge pressure to the oil column: $p = p_{\text{surface}} + \rho g h = 1.0 \times 10^{4} + 800 \times 9.8 \times 1.5 = 1.0 \times 10^{4} + 1.176 \times 10^{4} = 2.18 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$. The value $1.18 \times 10^{4}$ is the oil column alone, $1.00 \times 10^{4}$ forgets the column, and $9.41 \times 10^{3}$ uses water density by mistake without the surface term.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A hydraulic press has a small piston of area $0.010\ \text{m}^2$ and a large piston of area $0.50\ \text{m}^2$. A force of $200\ \text{N}$ is applied to the small piston. By Pascal's principle, what force appears on the large piston?
questions.explanation: Pascal's principle gives equal pressure on both pistons, so $F_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1) = 200 \times (0.50/0.010) = 200 \times 50 = 1.0 \times 10^{4}\ \text{N}$. The ratio of areas is the force multiplication. The value $4.0\ \text{N}$ divides by the ratio instead, $200\ \text{N}$ ignores the area difference, and $50\ \text{N}$ is the ratio alone.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A rectangular dam holds fresh water to a depth $H$ against a vertical face of width $b$. The average pressure acting on the wetted face is which of the following? Take $\rho$ for water and $g$ for gravity.
questions.explanation: Pressure rises linearly from $0$ at the surface to $\rho g H$ at the base, so its average over the depth is $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho g H$. The total force would be this average times area, $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho g H \cdot (Hb)$. The value $\rho g H$ is the maximum pressure not the average, $2\rho g H$ doubles it, and $\rho g H b$ is dimensionally a force per unit length, not a pressure.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A manometer uses oil of relative density $0.80$ (so $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 800\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$). A pressure difference produces an oil column height of $0.25\ \text{m}$. Taking $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what is the pressure difference?
questions.explanation: The head relation gives $\Delta p = \rho g h = 800 \times 9.8 \times 0.25 = 1.96 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Pa}$. Using water density $1000$ instead of $800$ gives $2.45 \times 10^{3}$, a power-of-ten slip gives $1.96 \times 10^{4}$, and $3.06 \times 10^{3}$ divides by $0.80$ rather than multiplying.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A steel ball of volume $2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3$ and mass $2.0\ \text{kg}$ is fully submerged and hangs from a spring scale in water of density $1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. Using $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, what does the scale read (the apparent weight)?
questions.explanation: The true weight is $W = mg = 2.0 \times 9.8 = 19.6\ \text{N}$. The buoyant force is $F_B = \rho g V = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 2.45\ \text{N}$. The scale reads $W - F_B = 19.6 - 2.45 = 17.2\ \text{N}$. The value $19.6\ \text{N}$ ignores buoyancy, $2.45\ \text{N}$ is the buoyant force alone, and $22.0\ \text{N}$ wrongly adds buoyancy to the weight.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: Pressure expressed as a height of fluid is called a head. To what gauge pressure does a head of $3.0\ \text{m}$ of water correspond? Take $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
questions.explanation: Converting head to pressure uses $p = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 3.0 = 2.94 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$. The value $9.8 \times 10^{3}$ uses a $1\ \text{m}$ head, $2.94 \times 10^{5}$ slips a power of ten, and $3.0 \times 10^{3}$ multiplies $\rho h$ without $g$.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A balloon of fixed volume $1.0\ \text{m}^3$ is filled with helium and held in air of density $1.2\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. Ignoring the mass of the envelope and the helium, what is the buoyant (upward) force from the surrounding air? Use $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
questions.explanation: Buoyancy works in gases too: $F_B = \rho_{\text{air}} g V = 1.2 \times 9.8 \times 1.0 = 11.8\ \text{N}$, the weight of the displaced air. The value $1.2\ \text{N}$ multiplies $\rho V$ without $g$, $9.8\ \text{N}$ uses unit air density, and $118\ \text{N}$ slips a power of ten.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A tank holds water of density $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. Take $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ and measure depth $h$ downward from the free surface.
questions.solution: Hydrostatic gauge pressure follows $p = \rho g h$. At a depth of $4.0\ \text{m}$ the gauge pressure is {{1}} $\text{Pa}$. Pressure in a still fluid {{2}} with depth, and at any fixed depth it is the same at every point on that horizontal {{3}}.
questions.explanation: With $h = 4.0\ \text{m}$, $p = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 4.0 = 39200\ \text{Pa}$. Because $\nabla p = \rho \mathbf{g}$ points downward, pressure increases with depth and is uniform across any horizontal level (an isobaric surface).
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A mercury manometer measures the gauge pressure of a gas. The density of mercury is $\rho = 13600\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
questions.solution: The gauge pressure equals the head of the mercury difference, $p = \rho g \Delta h$. For a difference of $\Delta h = 0.20\ \text{m}$ the gauge pressure is {{1}} $\text{Pa}$. If water were used instead of mercury, the same pressure would need a {{2}} column because water is much less dense.
questions.explanation: With $\Delta h = 0.20\ \text{m}$, $p = 13600 \times 9.8 \times 0.20 = 26656\ \text{Pa}$. Since $h = p/(\rho g)$, a smaller density needs a larger height for the same pressure, so a water manometer would need a taller column.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A solid block of volume $3.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$ is fully submerged in water of density $1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. Use $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
questions.solution: By Archimedes' principle the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced water, $F_B = \rho g V =$ {{1}} $\text{N}$. This force acts {{2}}. If the block's own weight is less than this buoyant force, the block will {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Here $F_B = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3} = 29.4\ \text{N}$, directed upward as the net effect of greater pressure on the lower face. If the weight is smaller than $F_B$, the net force is upward and the block rises.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A piece of wood of density $800\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ floats freely in water of density $1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$.
questions.solution: For a floating body the submerged volume fraction equals the density ratio $\rho_{\text{body}}/\rho_{\text{fluid}}$, which here is {{1}}. The fraction left above the surface is therefore {{2}}. If the same wood were placed in denser seawater, the submerged fraction would be {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The submerged fraction is $800/1000 = 0.8$, leaving $1 - 0.8 = 0.2$ above water. In denser seawater the denominator grows, so the ratio $\rho_{\text{body}}/\rho_{\text{fluid}}$ shrinks and less of the wood sits below the surface.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: An object weighs $W_{\text{air}} = 60\ \text{N}$ in air and $W_{\text{water}} = 40\ \text{N}$ when fully submerged in water of density $1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$. Use $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
questions.solution: The apparent loss of weight equals the buoyant force, $F_B = W_{\text{air}} - W_{\text{water}} =$ {{1}} $\text{N}$. The displaced volume is then $V = F_B/(\rho g) =$ {{2}} $\text{m}^3$ (to three significant figures).
questions.explanation: The buoyant force is $60 - 40 = 20\ \text{N}$. Then $V = F_B/(\rho g) = 20/(1000 \times 9.8) = 2.04 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$, the volume of the object since it is fully submerged.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics

questions.stem: A closed tank has air above water held at a gauge pressure of $p_0 = 5.0 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Pa}$. The water has density $1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ and $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
questions.solution: Pressure adds along the depth, $p = p_0 + \rho g h$. At a depth of $2.0\ \text{m}$ below the water surface the gauge pressure is {{1}} $\text{Pa}$. Compared with an open tank at the same depth, this pressure is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The water column contributes $\rho g h = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 2.0 = 19600\ \text{Pa}$, and adding the surface gauge pressure gives $5000 + 19600 = 24600\ \text{Pa}$. An open tank would have only the $19600\ \text{Pa}$ column, so the pressurized tank reads higher.
questions.source: in the style of Acheson, Elementary Fluid Dynamics
